old_claim.note: Military Field Medicine provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim_absent: No direct military field medicine dependency remains because the node is scoped to Listerian antiseptic and sterile surgical practice, not battlefield evacuation or triage systems.

source_shape.source_locator: King's College London states that Lister first successfully used his antiseptic method in 1865 and published the system in The Lancet in 1867.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source pins aseptic surgery to Listerian antiseptic practice rather than to military field-care organization.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Field medicine remains a separate emergency-care lineage; keeping it direct here would turn a care setting into the mechanism of asepsis.

ontology_before: Aseptic Surgery depended on Military Field Medicine as an unsourced enabling edge.
ontology_after: Aseptic Surgery depends on clinical medicine, anesthesia, and chemistry while military field systems remain outside its direct prerequisite set.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: military_field_medicine is no longer a direct prerequisite for aseptic_surgery.
would_reject_if: The node were rescoped to battlefield antiseptic protocols rather than surgical asepsis generally.
A stronger source showed Listerian asepsis directly depended on military field-medicine institutions.
A separate military_aseptic_surgery node were added for wartime surgical practice.
short_reason: Field medicine is care context, not asepsis mechanism.
